mnemonics.title: OTP Requirements - TRUS
mnemonics.content:
T - Truly Random key
R - Same length as message (Required length)
U - Used Only Once
S - Secret (kept private)

mnemonics.title: Remember XOR Properties - SASS
mnemonics.content:
S - Self-inverse: A⊕A=0
A - Additive identity: A⊕0=A
S - Symmetric: A⊕B=B⊕A
S - Shift invariant: (A⊕B)⊕C=A⊕(B⊕C)

mnemonics.title: Key Reuse Danger - T-PAD
mnemonics.content:
T - Two-time pad breaks security
P - Plaintext relationship exposed
A - Attacker XORs C1⊕C2
D - Decrypts relationship not individual messages

acronyms.expansion: One-Time Pad - A symmetric cipher using random key equal to message length, used once only
acronyms.usage: Used in military communications, diplomatic channels, and highly sensitive data transmission where perfect secrecy is required

acronyms.expansion: Exclusive OR - Boolean operation where output is true only when inputs differ
acronyms.usage: Fundamental operation in OTP encryption: C = P ⊕ K, P = C ⊕ K

acronyms.expansion: Pseudo-Random Number Generator - Deterministic algorithm producing sequence from seed
acronyms.usage: INSUFFICIENT for OTP - use TRNG (True Random Number Generator) instead for perfect secrecy

acronyms.expansion: True Random Number Generator - Hardware-based randomness from physical phenomena
acronyms.usage: Required for OTP key generation to achieve perfect secrecy; captures thermal noise, radio noise, or quantum effects

visualTips.visualization:
Show plaintext bits [0,1,1,0,1,0] above key bits [1,0,1,1,0,1] with XOR result below:
0⊕1=1, 1⊕0=1, 1⊕1=0, 0⊕1=1, 1⊕0=1, 0⊕1=1 → ciphertext [1,1,0,1,1,1]
Demonstrate self-inverse: XOR ciphertext with same key recovers plaintext
Create a probability tree showing: Given any ciphertext C, there exists a valid key K' for EVERY possible plaintext P'. This means Pr[P|C] = Pr[P] - ciphertext reveals nothing new about plaintext.
Draw two ciphertext boxes C1 and C2, show them being XORed together, with key K canceling out (K⊕K=0), leaving P1⊕P2 exposed - demonstrating why reuse is fatal.

analogies.analogy: OTP is like a one-time combination lock where: the lock code (key) is random, as long as the lock mechanism (message), used only once, and known only to sender/receiver. Once used, the lock code is destroyed.

XOR is like a light switch toggle: applying the same toggle twice returns to original state (0⊕0=0, 1⊕1=0). It's also like mixing paint: adding black (key) to white gives black; adding black again returns to white.

OTP key distribution is like needing to deliver a safe (containing the key) that's as large as the message itself. You need secure delivery of the safe before you can send the message - making OTP impractical for most modern communication.
